speaker: interviewer
text: Herling. Paula, you told me you moved to Germany to find your brother. Before you went there, I wanted to ask you, in Lodz, did you receive any assistance from the Joint or the Red Cross, or any other organization after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just-- they had a committee, and I went to register that I am alive, in case somebody asked for me. So we all did, and registered for that. And I think over there I found out about my brother. So I-- I said, I'm not going to stay here. I'm going to see my brother. He was the oldest brother. And he lived in Kielce, so he was liberated in Germany. He-- he had stories to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe Herling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paula, how did you get from Lodz to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: A person smuggled at us at night. There were a few people, and they smuggled us to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you had been caught, what would have been your fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would be sent back to Poland, probably, because it was after the war, and they couldn't shoot us. But we would-- we would be sent back. And we didn't know how to cross the-- the-- the [? drainage-- ?] how is it-- how to cross by ourselves. We had to have a guard. So we-- we were a few people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived in Feldafing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? What date? What year, what time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was winter time. Winter time. And I went to Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year? In 1945 or '46?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that reunion with your brother like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very happy. We cried, we laughed, and we started telling stories to each other, how we survived. And we were very happy from then on. He lived-- live with the men barrack, and I went-- he arranged for me to live in another barrack with ladies. And after a while, he had a friend, a girlfriend, who I got to know. And they got married in Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a wedding in Feldafing like without family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without family, nothing. It was-- they went to a rabbi. There was a new camp rabbi, and they had the ceremony performed. They went home. They had a supper, probably. We had a supper together. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you remain in Feldafing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a special job or function there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I worked right away in central [INAUDIBLE], in-- in the UNRRA, where they had to have all the particulars from the-- how many people, and they-- and they distributed the goods for them, the food. And I worked there all the time since I came. But I didn't have-- my sister from-- lived in Germany, too, my sister Sally. So we went to visit each other where she lived. My sister Chajka lived also in another city. We visited. But they were all married. They had places where to go. Sally went to the States. My sister Chajka went to Israel. She didn't have anybody, so she was remarried in Lodz. She went to Israel with a son, which got killed in 1948 right away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't have anybody. Bella didn't have anybody. So we went-- both registered. They had-- we had protection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I worked there, and that's in [INAUDIBLE]. And then they had from Canada came a request for domestics. So I arranged it for domestic, I and my sister Bella, to-- to get out from Germany. I didn't want to stay in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you what it was like living in Germany among Germans. What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- when we-- where we were living, no Germans were living in Feldafing. We didn't see them. These were all big villas where the German soldiers were living before the war. That was like a-- a rest port, a rest place for them, like a holiday, they went out. There was a beautiful villa, a beautiful place. And when we went out to Munchen, it was not far from there, so we only went out to a theater or to a-- to see something good, a Jewish-- Jewish get together. And that's all. But most of the time, I was in Feldafing, and I couldn't go wherever I wanted because I had a job. It wasn't a paid job, but I wanted to work. So when they had that request for domestics, they gave me the-- the first. And so we went as a domestic. I and my sister went to German, to Munchen, to the consulate. And they didn't take my sister, only me. Because my sister, they said, is too delicate and too beautiful to be a domestic. So she remained, and I went. And she was crying terribly. But I said, remember, Bella, when I went-- we'll be there. It won't take long, and I'll take you over there to me. And that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you arrive in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in Canada in April 7, I think, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the response of the local Jewish community towards you as a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here? I don't know. I was with one family. I was a domestic. I worked and was here with one family looking after a child. That was-- and they were very nice to me-- beautiful people. Beautiful people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain there?

speaker: interviewee
text: One year. I had a contract.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I met a-- on a party, I met a-- a young man. And he was invited to that party from other people, and I was from another party invited. And we met there. Somebody introduced us. And we were only the two [? greener. ?] So we met, and from then on, my husband, by the second time we met, he said, I would like you-- to marry-- to marry you. The second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shlomo Mintzmacher. And I said to that, we need two people to tango. [LAUGHS] You want, but I don't-- I'm not ready yet. But anyway, after quite a while-- took us a year. I had this contract. But she let me before the contract, after 10 months. And she gave me a note of a whole year. They were very beautiful people. Like a family, I was there. They treated me beautiful. I didn't do much. She prepared dinners. And I was out with the little guy, little boy, Peter, take care of him. We came home. She was a wonderful lady. But unfortunately, they both died. They are not here. They have two-- three sons. One son is the famous, if you know him, from Bram, Sharon, and Lois. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You don't know the-- the-- they play, musicians. Bram, that was one of the sons. And he comes to me sometimes. He visits me. He's a nice man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband was a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a survivor, but being in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it possible to make a new life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here? It was very hard, especially for me, and for him because he was very poor-- very poor. He didn't have anything. And I had some salary, $10 a month, I think. And [INAUDIBLE], so I still saved the $10. From the $10 I saved $5. He didn't have one penny. So he asked me to lend him some money for-- for a wedding ring. So I did. And this is the wedding ring I wear still. And we started a new life. We got married here. The marriage consisted of five families, by-- downstairs in the hazzan's basement. And a cousin of mine living here, old-timer, she made the supper for us in her house. And that's all. We rented a flat, and somebody gave us a note to go to a furniture store and buy some proper furniture. We didn't have money, so he-- he-- he took his word for us. And we lived on the flat. Then we had to go to work right away. Three years I was working together with my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what? What kind of business did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: If he was working, he was working as a fur finisher. And I was working in a factory where they gave me [INAUDIBLE] not [INAUDIBLE] but [INAUDIBLE] finishing. I never-- I hated-- sew-- sewing. But I had to do it. They taught me, and I got it. And from then on, I worked in another factory, examining umbrellas. And I couldn't-- while I was with these people, I went to school, the Harbor School, at night time. They brought me to school. They picked me up. And I learned the language, although I came-- when I came, I did know a little bit of English. Because I-- she-- they didn't know any Yiddish, so I had to communicate with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I did a little bit English. I took lessons in Germany, when I was there. And here, I was even the radio. At the school finish, I was on the radio saying that I'm a newcomer, and I learned the language, and I performed it very good. OK? And how did you establish a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well-- I worked very hard. After four years-- three years, my daughter, first daughter was born. We borrowed some money from my family, from my sister. She was married. I-- I got her married here. And we bought the first house. A three-- three-story house-- rented all the-- all the rooms, as many as I had. I had only one-- one kitchen and one bedroom, and the rest was all rented with tenants. We lived with them pretty good. I treated them good, and they stayed with me. In a short time, I sold that house and went to a newer house up north. But my husband didn't make a living. He was more unemployed than employed. Wherever he came to, whichever factory he came, they said, no, he's not a worker. He's a professor. He shouldn't be with us. And they sent him off. He was a very long time unemployed. Only in the hottest seasons he worked a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children did you have in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had after Sally, I had Irving and I had Anne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are they all married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now they're all married, and they have their own children. And I am a grandmother of eight grandchildren. God bless them. And real nice people, I have to say that. Beautiful--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened-- what happened with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband had a stroke in 1982, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and not a very hard stroke, a light stroke. But he was-- he was having trouble seeing. And he was a different person, so I had to look after him for a while. For eight years, nine years, since 1991, he couldn't walk. So the doctors told him he had to have an operation on his legs. He went into the hospital. The operation didn't cure yet. And he got a massive heart-- a massive stroke, and he died. It took only 24 hours. That's how I lost him, the 28th of-- of December, 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paula, I want to thank you very much for the testimony you have given us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in conclusion, I wanted to ask you some final questions. In what way did your experiences during the shoah affect you as a parent, as a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a parent, I can tell you that whatever I went through, I told you, not in such particular way, but in a shorter way, I explained it to my children. And they know what I went through and what a mother they had. I was very happy to have the children. I wanted to have the children, because we lost so many children of ours, the Jewish children in the world, [? what ?] they did [? to them. ?] I had one child, my Sally, and the doctor said I couldn't have another-- any other children. I went through a big operation in order to have some more. Sally shouldn't be by herself. And I did. I succeeded. I had two more, and I was very happy. And they know that my life and father's life wasn't easy. And they are 100% good people-- the real meaning of the word. Nice people, and I'm very proud of them. And for me, it made a [INAUDIBLE] terrible, shocking experience. And I hope and I wish that something like this will not occur, not just here, but in the whole world. And my daughter Anne, she said, here is not the place for her. She wants to go to Israel. And she did. She said to me plain and simple, mother, I love it here. They just don't need the money only in Israel. They need young people. Let Irvin come, let Sally come, and you can come too. I see the good sides in Israel and the bad sides in Israel, but I love it here. And I can't help it. Let her be there. And I go to her, visit her every year, almost. And I'm very happy to be with them. She has a beautiful family, a good husband, and she's happy. Thanks God for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Paula.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my dear father-- pardon me-- Itzhak Meyer Herlink from Suchedniow. This picture was taken in Suchedniow, but I don't know exactly the date. But that's all what I can tell you about him. He was a wonderful, good man, and I don't know why this happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: This he was taken before the war, Paula?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken before the war, sometimes before the war. Maybe a long time before the war. This is my dear mother, Hannah Rivka [? Stern ?] Herlink. This picture was taken before the war, a long time, when she got married to my father. In Radom is the picture taken. And she perished in Treblinka, too. She was a wonderful mother and a good wife. And a good-- taking care of eight children. I will never forget her. The picture on the bottom right is before the war, my mother with four of us girls and one stepsister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The names are my mother, Hannah Rivka, my oldest sister, Miriam Esther, my youngest sister, Bella, my second youngest, [? Manja, ?] and [? Pesa, ?] and Chajka is standing on-- behind my mother. The second picture is made in Radom before the war. I am there with my two girlfriends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you, Paula?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the middle one. My second picture, third picture is my father and mother with my two sisters, Miriam Esther and Nacha. All perished in Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was that picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Suchedniow before the war. Exactly the date, I don't remember, but it must've been around '37 or '38. The next picture is at home taken. I am in the left. My sister Nacha is the middle, and my oldest sister is Miriam Esther, the right. These two sisters perished also together with my parents. The last but not the least is my dear father, Itzhak Meyer, and my mother, Hannah Rivka, taken pictures by the Germans for their needs. They wanted to have them all register with pictures. This picture was taken in Suchedniow during the war. The Germans took us to work. We gather here hay in a field. So I am here, the second from the right with the rake. And beside me, the first one is a German soldier, and the second from me is a Jewish man. His name is [? Ferman ?]. And the rest are also Jewish girls. I don't remember their names. But the German made that picture special to give it to us. So I kept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in summer of 1941. This is a picture also from Suchedniow, taken in year 1941, where we gathered the hay, and now we are loading it on the trucks, on the wagons. The same German took that picture in Suchedniow in summertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture taken in Lodz in August 19, 1995. '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me, 1945. On the left, is my sister-- no, sorry, can you erase that? On the right is my brother-in-law, [? Tadek ?] Buchman, and sister Sally Herling getting married. This picture is in the same day as they got married. Besides Sarah is my younger sister, Bella Herling Abramowitz. In the middle between my sister Sarah and my brother-in-law, is-- this is myself, Paula Herling Mintz. And this was a very joyous occasion between us, because nobody else was there to attend our wedding. Our parents weren't there, unfortunately. They perished. And this was the first wedding after the war, by-- by my sister, Sally Herling Buchman. This picture is taken in 1946, in Munchen, Germany. This is myself, Paula Herling Mintz, and my sister, Bella Herling Abramowitz, living in Montreal now. And we were always together, like friends and sisters. Very good friend of mine. Thank you. Here I work in the [INAUDIBLE] in-- from UNRRA, in Feldafing in year 1946. As soon as I came to Feldafing, I went to work. And I loved it, to be there. This is one of my pictures there. I have some others, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, right. Now we want--

speaker: interviewer
text: There's two here. We have more pictures here. I don't know who is this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's their wedding day. Got to have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and bring all those.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there will be my daughter, my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So that's the wedding date. How does that come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful. You see, this is so-- when you get your copy, then you can have three copies made, one for each child. I think it's important, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one for me. [LAUGHS] I've got quite a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, survivors have phoned me and made a copy for me. Yeah. It's not the same quality as what you get from California, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Back lights-- OK, ready?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. This is a picture from my wedding, taking place in Toronto, January 30, 1949. And my husband name is Shlomo Mintzmacher, and mine was Paula Herling. That was taken at the photographer in Toronto. And the wedding took place at the hazzan's place, in a basement. And his name was Hazzan [? Harloff. ?] That is my oldest daughter, Sally Mintz Levy, and her husband, Gilbert Levy, taken place in Toronto. These are my daughter Sally's children. The oldest is Aaron Mintz-- Aaron Levy, Hart Levy, and David Levy.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left is--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left one is Aaron. The middle is Hart. The youngest is David. These are all pictures taken last year in their school which they attend, Bialik Hebrew Day School.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the year--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son Irvin, Hebrew name Itzhak Mayer, after my late father, with his wife, Cora Dobyns, now Cora Mintz, and with their first daughter, Danya Mintz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where and when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Toronto in about 1982. These are my three grandchildren from my son, Irvin Mintz. From the left is their son, Jared Mintz. The small one is Noah Mintz, the youngest, and Danya as you saw in the other picture. This is my youngest daughter, Anne Rachel Mintz, with her husband, [? Yossi ?] Cohen, and her son, Itamar Cohen. He is one and 1/2 here. And here is the oldest daughter, Shani. She's 5 years old. They live in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I am with my husband, Shlomo Mintz. Taken-- this picture was taken in 1988 at home to have a portrait with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't any special occasion, but I just wanted to have him with a portrait. Thank you.
